items.q: Most movements in the human body use which class of lever?
items.why: Students assume the most familiar (first class - seesaw) is most common. Actually most body levers are third class (effort between fulcrum and load) - like the biceps curl. They sacrifice mechanical advantage for speed/range.

items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.

items.correct: Volume of blood pumped by the heart per minute (stroke volume x heart rate).
items.misconception: The volume of blood in the heart at any moment.
items.why: Students confuse stroke volume (one beat) with cardiac output (per minute total). CO = SV x HR. A 70mL stroke at 70bpm = 4.9L/min cardiac output.

items.q: Moment of force is calculated as:
items.why: It's Force × perpendicular distance to fulcrum. “Force / distance” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Sum of moments about any point = 0
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sum of moments about any point = 0. “Sum of forces = mass × acceleration” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If a 100 N weight hangs 0.4 m from elbow and biceps inserts 0.05 m from elbow, biceps force (N) required is?
items.why: Exact answer: 800. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
